meta.title: Get to know the characters
meta.eyebrow: আসুন, চরিত্রগুলোকে একটু কাছ থেকে জানা যাক।
meta.intro: Taranath Tantrik stories weave together occult practitioners, restless spirits, and ordinary people caught in the supernatural. These are the figures you will meet again and again across the archive.

characters.tagline: An occult practitioner who recounts adventures from his past
characters.bio: A mystic figure and practitioner of occult, who lives with his daughter Chari in a house on Mott Lane in central Kolkata, after having lost most of his supernatural powers because of misuse of Tantra. He is described as a man in his mid-fifties, an astrologer by profession, who has had many encounters with the supernatural in his extensive travels throughout Bengal. He shares his life experiences with two young friends - an unnamed writer (often read as Bibhutibhushan Bandyopadhyay himself, and later his son Taradas) and Kishori Sen - at his Mott Lane home over cups of tea and a pack of Passing Show cigarettes.
characters.appearance: An ageing, worldly man - unremarkable at first glance, but with a certain air of mystery and wisdom when he begins to speak.

characters.tagline: The quiet anchor of the Mott Lane household
characters.bio: Chari is Taranath's only daughter and constant companion in his old house on Mott Lane. After the death of her mother, she keeps the household running while her father retreats into astrology, memory, and the occult. In Bibhutibhushan's frame narrative she is described as a pretty but strange young woman - practical where Taranath is abstract, present yet slightly apart from the adda of tea, cigarettes, and ghost stories that unfolds in their parlour. She rarely speaks in the stories themselves, but her presence defines the domestic world from which Taranath's tales depart.
characters.appearance: A young woman of unsettling beauty - often in the background of the Mott Lane rooms, watchful and composed.

characters.tagline: The friend who visits with cigarettes and scepticism
characters.bio: Kishori Sen is one of the two young men who regularly visit Taranath's Mott Lane home. Together with the unnamed writer-narrator, he brings Passing Show cigarettes and prompts the tantrik to recount his past - journeys across rural Bengal, encounters with spirits, gurus, and forces beyond ordinary understanding. Kishori and the writer are rationalists by temperament; they doubt, question, and sometimes mock what they hear, yet they return again and again. Their visits supply the frame for nearly every story in the canon: rain over Kolkata, tea on the table, and Taranath's voice drawing them into another world.
characters.appearance: A young Bengali man of the city - curious, informal, more comfortable with doubt than with devotion.

characters.tagline: The apparition by the Barakar river
characters.bio: Madhusundari Devi appears in one of Bibhutibhushan's two foundational Taranath stories, Madhusundari Debi'r Abirbhab. Taranath recalls a period of three months spent beside the Barakar river in western Bengal, where he called her forth as a lover in a desolate landscape of winter water, moonlight, and forest. She visited him every night - a presence he describes not as an ordinary woman but as a goddess granting joy and companionship beyond anything earthly. When the idyll ended, the loss left him unable to recover the self that had known such happiness. Her story is among the most remembered in the entire series, and a gateway for many readers into Taranath's world.
characters.appearance: Known through memory and longing rather than a fixed portrait - associated with river-banks, moonlit winter, and an intimacy that feels heavenly rather than human.

contextVideo.title: About Madhusundari Devi
contextVideo.description: Taranath recounts how he came to know her beside the Barakar river - the story that first brought her into the canon.

characters.tagline: The mysterious woman of Birbhum who initiated Taranath
characters.bio: Matu Pagli is Taranath's first guru in the art of tantra - a figure he recalls while describing his early struggles to learn occult practice. Associated with Birbhum in western Bengal, she appears in the sub-narrative embedded within Taranath Tantrik-er Golpo and was later expanded in adaptations by Taradas Bandyopadhyay and the Hoichoi web series. Her name suggests both motherhood and madness: a woman at the margins of society who holds knowledge the respectable world refuses. Taranath's encounter with her marks the beginning of a lifelong entanglement with powers he would never fully master.
characters.appearance: A formidable rural woman - unsettling, authoritative, and tied to the landscapes of Birbhum rather than the parlours of Kolkata.

contextVideo.title: About Matu Pagli
contextVideo.description: Taranath recounts how he came to know her in Birbhum.

characters.tagline: The nameless man beneath the chotka tree in Amar Jiban
characters.bio: In Taradas Bandyopadhyay's Amar Jiban, Taranath recalls a boyhood afternoon at the edge of his village - a blazing field by Koi Khal where a bare-chested man sat beneath a chotka tree, tending pigs that were not ordinary swine but the outward form of human failings. He told the child he had been sent to meet him alone, read his forehead, and opened a vision of cosmic creation through his unbearably bright eyes - leaving Taranath with a melancholy he would never lose. Years later, when a tantric guru studied Taranath's palm, he said the boy had already met a liberated mahapurush without recognising him. Taranath thought at once of this stranger.
characters.appearance: A dark, compact man of startling vitality - bare-bodied, bone wand in hand, eyes too brilliant to hold; outwardly no different from any village swineherd until he speaks.
characters.imageAlt: The swineherd mystic of Koi Khal

contextVideo.title: Amar Jiban
contextVideo.description: Taranath recounts the childhood encounter that marked his life as unlike any other - Taradas Bandyopadhyay's Amar Jiban, adapted for Mirchi Bangla.
